---
title: "LLM Integration Plan"
subtitle: "CRPD Disability Rights Data Dashboard"
author:
  - name: "Institute on Disability and Public Policy (IDPP)"
    affiliation: "American University"
date: today
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-expand: 2
    number-sections: true
    theme: [cosmo, custom.scss]
    highlight-style: github
    code-fold: show
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    embed-resources: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    fig-align: center
    link-external-icon: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
    callout-appearance: simple
    page-layout: article

execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false

---

```{=html}
<style>
  body { font-family: "Inter", "Helvetica Neue", sans-serif; }
  h1, h2, h3 { font-weight: 700; }
  .callout { border-radius: 6px; }
  table { font-size: 0.9em; }
  thead { background-color: #f0f4ff; }
  .badge {
    display: inline-block;
    padding: 2px 8px;
    border-radius: 12px;
    font-size: 0.75em;
    font-weight: 600;
  }
  .badge-green  { background: #d1fae5; color: #065f46; }
  .badge-blue   { background: #dbeafe; color: #1e40af; }
  .badge-yellow { background: #fef9c3; color: #854d0e; }
  .pipeline-step {
    border-left: 4px solid #667eea;
    padding: 0.6rem 1rem;
    margin: 0.5rem 0;
    background: #f8f9ff;
    border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
  }
</style>
```

---

# Executive Summary {.unnumbered}

This document defines the complete technical strategy for integrating Large Language Models
(LLMs) into the CRPD Disability Rights Data Dashboard. The dashboard currently serves
researchers, policymakers, and disability rights advocates by surfacing patterns across
**585 documents from 155 countries (2010–2026)** using rule-based keyword analytics.

LLM integration will transform the platform from a *data viewer* into a *research assistant*
— enabling natural language queries, document-grounded answers, AI-generated policy briefs,
and semantic search.

::: {.callout-important}
## Critical Pre-Condition

The existing `clean_text` column in `crpd_reports.csv` has all punctuation stripped and all
text lowercased — it was designed for regex keyword matching, not LLM input. **The original
PDF source files must be re-processed** through a proper conversion pipeline before LLM
integration can proceed.
:::

## Chosen Approach

The full pipeline, run **once offline**, produces a lightweight search index that powers all
user-facing LLM features:

<div class="pipeline-step">📥 <strong>Step 1 — Collect</strong> &nbsp; Download 585 PDFs from UN Treaty Body Database</div>
<div class="pipeline-step">📄 <strong>Step 2 — Convert</strong> &nbsp; PDF → structured Markdown (pymupdf4llm)</div>
<div class="pipeline-step">✂️ <strong>Step 3 — Chunk</strong> &nbsp; Split documents into ~500-word semantic segments</div>
<div class="pipeline-step">🔢 <strong>Step 4 — Embed</strong> &nbsp; Vectorize chunks (sentence-transformers, free, local)</div>
<div class="pipeline-step">🗃️ <strong>Step 5 — Index</strong> &nbsp; Store in FAISS for millisecond retrieval</div>
<div class="pipeline-step">💬 <strong>Step 6 — Query</strong> &nbsp; RAG: retrieve relevant chunks → answer with Ollama llama3 / Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile</div>

---

# Project Context

## Current Dashboard

The CRPD Disability Rights Data Dashboard is a **Streamlit** application deployed on Posit
Cloud ([idpp.connect.posit.cloud/crpd-dashboard](https://idpp.connect.posit.cloud/crpd-dashboard)).

### Analytics Stack & Limitations

| Component | Current Technology | Limitation |
|-----------|------------------|------------|
| Text matching | Regex + word boundaries | Exact match only; misses synonyms |
| Article frequency | Keyword counting | No semantic understanding |
| Topic modeling | LDA (scikit-learn) | No interpretable labels |
| Classification | Hardcoded lexicons | Binary; no nuance or context |
| Document search | String `contains` | No semantic similarity |

### Corpus Statistics

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Total documents | 585 |
| Countries covered | 155 |
| Temporal range | 2010 – 2026 |
| Language | English (all documents) |
| Average document length | 11,071 words |
| Longest document | 69,735 words |
| Document types | 5 (State Reports, LOI, Written Replies, Concluding Observations, Responses) |

## What LLM Integration Adds

| New Capability | User Benefit |
|----------------|-------------|
| Natural language Q&A | Ask questions in plain English; receive grounded answers |
| Semantic search | Find relevant documents beyond exact keyword matching |
| AI narrative summaries | Plain-English explanation of any filtered view |
| Policy brief generation | One-click downloadable research briefs |
| Document-grounded RAG | Answers citing specific countries, years, and document types |

---

# System Architecture

## Full Pipeline Diagram

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                  OFFLINE  (run once, ~30 minutes)               ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                  ║
║  UN Digital Library API                                          ║
║  digitallibrary.un.org  ──────────────────────────────────────► ║
║                                                                  ║
║  download_pdfs.py        sync_new_documents.py                   ║
║  (initial 585 PDFs)      (monthly auto-update)                   ║
║         │                        │                               ║
║         └──────────┬─────────────┘                               ║
║                    ▼                                             ║
║            data/pdfs/   (585 PDF files)                          ║
║                    │                                             ║
║                    ▼  pymupdf4llm                                ║
║            data/markdown/   (585 .md files)                      ║
║                    │                                             ║
║                    ▼  chunk_by_section()                         ║
║            ~15,000 chunks  (500 words / 75-word overlap)         ║
║                    │                                             ║
║                    ▼  sentence-transformers                      ║
║            data/embeddings.npy   (15000 × 768 float32)           ║
║                    │                                             ║
║                    ▼  FAISS IndexFlatIP                          ║
║            data/faiss_index.bin  (~20 MB)                        ║
║            data/chunks_metadata.json                             ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
                         │
                committed to repo / Posit Cloud
                         │
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                  ONLINE  (per user request)                     ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                  ║
║  User types question in Streamlit chat                           ║
║         │                                                        ║
║         ▼  sentence-transformers (local, free)                   ║
║  Question embedding (768-dim vector)                             ║
║         │                                                        ║
║         ▼  FAISS nearest-neighbor search  (<5 ms)               ║
║  Top 6–8 relevant document chunks                                ║
║         │                                                        ║
║         ▼  Build prompt                                          ║
║  [system prompt] + [data context] + [retrieved chunks]           ║
║         │                                                        ║
║         ▼  Ollama llama3 (local) / Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile   ║
║  Grounded answer with citations                                  ║
║  (country, year, document type)                                  ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

## Repository Structure

```
crpd-dashboard/
├── app.py
├── requirements.txt                  ← add 4 packages
├── .streamlit/
│   └── secrets.toml                  ← GROQ_API_KEY (git-ignored)
├── data/
│   ├── crpd_reports.csv              ← existing
│   ├── pdfs/                         ← NEW: 585 source PDFs
│   ├── markdown/                     ← NEW: 585 converted .md files
│   ├── embeddings.npy                ← NEW: ~25 MB
│   ├── faiss_index.bin               ← NEW: ~20 MB
│   └── chunks_metadata.json          ← NEW: chunk text + metadata
├── LLM_Development/
│   ├── CRPD_LLM_Integration_Plan.qmd ← this document
│   ├── download_pdfs.py              ← initial PDF collection
│   ├── sync_new_documents.py         ← monthly auto-sync
│   └── build_knowledge_base.py       ← PDF → index pipeline
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       └── sync_crpd.yml             ← GitHub Actions monthly schedule
└── src/
    ├── llm.py                        ← NEW: all LLM/RAG logic
    ├── tab_chat.py                   ← NEW: AI Research Assistant standalone page (page == "chat")
    ├── tab_analyze.py                ← unmodified for chat; still hosts Article Coverage, Model Shift, etc.
    ├── tab_overview.py               ← modified: AI Insights panel
    └── tab_explore.py                ← modified: Report generation (Phase 4)
```

---

# Step 1 — Document Collection

## Data Source

All 585 documents originate from the **UN Treaty Body Database**
([tbinternet.ohchr.org](https://tbinternet.ohchr.org)). Every document is publicly available
with no login required.

::: {.callout-note}
## No Official API Exists

The UN Treaty Body Database (`tbinternet.ohchr.org`) has no public API, RSS feed, or bulk
download endpoint. However, two mechanisms make automated collection possible without
web scraping.
:::

## Mechanism 1 — Direct Download by Symbol

Every document in `crpd_reports.csv` has a `symbol` column (e.g., `CRPD_C_ITA_1`).
The UN download server accepts these symbols directly via URL:

```
https://tbinternet.ohchr.org/_layouts/treatybodyexternal/Download.aspx
  ?symbolno=CRPD%2FC%2FITA%2F1&Lang=en
```

Conversion from CSV symbol to URL is straightforward:

```python
def symbol_to_url(symbol: str) -> str:
    # CRPD_C_ITA_1  →  CRPD/C/ITA/1  →  CRPD%2FC%2FITA%2F1
    slash_form = symbol.replace("_", "/")
    encoded    = slash_form.replace("/", "%2F")
    return f"https://tbinternet.ohchr.org/_layouts/treatybodyexternal/Download.aspx?symbolno={encoded}&Lang=en"
```

**Script:** `LLM_Development/download_pdfs.py`

| Command | Action |
|---------|--------|
| `python download_pdfs.py --dry-run` | Preview downloads without fetching |
| `python download_pdfs.py` | Download all 585 PDFs (~20 min) |
| `python download_pdfs.py --resume` | Skip already-downloaded files |
| `python download_pdfs.py --limit 10` | Test with first 10 documents |

## Mechanism 2 — Automated Monthly Sync via UN Digital Library API

The **UN Digital Library** (`digitallibrary.un.org`) provides a documented, free JSON API
that indexes all CRPD documents and is updated as new submissions arrive.

**API endpoint:**

```
GET https://digitallibrary.un.org/search
    ?p=CRPD/C
    &of=recjson
    &sf=year
    &so=d
    &rg=100
```

This returns structured JSON including document symbols, publication dates, country
information, and direct PDF download links. A query run in March 2026 returned documents
published as recently as **January 2026**, confirming the API reflects current submissions.

**Sync script:** `LLM_Development/sync_new_documents.py`

```python
# Core logic: query API → compare against known symbols → download new
records = fetch_all_library_records("CRPD/C")
known_symbols = set(df_existing["symbol"])
new_records = [r for r in records if r["symbol_csv"] not in known_symbols]
```

**GitHub Actions schedule** (`.github/workflows/sync_crpd.yml`):

```yaml
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 1 * *'   # 6 AM UTC, 1st of every month
  workflow_dispatch:        # manual trigger from GitHub UI
```

Every month, GitHub automatically:

1. Queries the UN Digital Library API
2. Identifies new documents not yet in `crpd_reports.csv`
3. Downloads the new PDFs
4. Converts them to Markdown
5. Appends new rows to the CSV
6. Re-builds the FAISS knowledge base
7. Commits all updated files back to the repository

---

# Step 2 — PDF to Markdown Conversion

## Why This Step is Necessary

The existing `clean_text` column was processed for NLP keyword matching — all punctuation
removed, all text lowercased. It is broken for LLM use. Structure (article headings,
paragraph references, numbered recommendations) has been lost entirely. Re-processing the
original PDFs restores all of this.

## Library Comparison

### `pymupdf4llm` — Recommended {.badge-green}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Best default choice for CRPD documents
:::

```{python}
import pymupdf4llm

md_text = pymupdf4llm.to_markdown("Italy_2013_CRPD_C_ITA_1.pdf")
# Returns clean Markdown with preserved headings, paragraphs, and tables
```

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Output quality | Excellent for digital PDFs |
| Speed | ~0.5 seconds per document |
| Preserves | Headings, paragraphs, columns, tables |
| OCR | Not needed (UN documents are native digital) |
| Install | `pip install pymupdf4llm` |

---

### `docling` (IBM Research) — Best Quality

```{python}
from docling.document_converter import DocumentConverter

converter = DocumentConverter()
result = converter.convert("Italy_2013_CRPD_C_ITA_1.pdf")
md_text = result.document.export_to_markdown()
```

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Output quality | Best available (AI-based layout analysis) |
| Speed | 5–15 seconds per document |
| Preserves | Everything, including complex layouts |
| OCR | Built-in (Surya) |
| Install | `pip install docling` (~500 MB models on first run) |
| Use when | pymupdf4llm output is insufficient on a sample check |

---

### Library Summary

| Library | Quality | Speed | OCR | Recommended Use |
|---------|:-------:|:-----:|:---:|-----------------|
| **pymupdf4llm** | ★★★★☆ | ★★★★★ | No | **Default — start here** |
| **docling** | ★★★★★ | ★★☆☆☆ | Yes | Fallback if quality insufficient |
| marker | ★★★★☆ | ★★☆☆☆ | Yes | Scanned documents only |
| markitdown | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★☆ | No | Multi-format pipelines |
| pdfplumber | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★☆ | No | Only if full control needed |

---

# Step 3 — Text Chunking

## Why Chunking is Necessary

CRPD documents average 11,071 words (~14,400 tokens). Even with large LLM context
windows, passing entire documents per query is wasteful and noisy. Chunking splits each
document into focused segments so the vector search can retrieve only the most relevant
portions.

With 585 documents × ~26 chunks each, the full index contains approximately
**15,000 chunks** — a very manageable size for FAISS on CPU.

## Recommended Strategy — Semantic Chunking

Split at natural Markdown heading boundaries produced by pymupdf4llm, falling back to
fixed-size windows for very long sections.

```{python}
import re

def chunk_by_section(md_text: str, max_words: int = 500,
                     overlap: int = 75) -> list[str]:
    """Split Markdown at heading boundaries; fixed fallback for long sections."""
    sections = re.split(r'\n(?=#{1,3} )', md_text)
    chunks, buffer = [], ""

    for section in sections:
        combined = (buffer + "\n" + section).strip() if buffer else section.strip()
        if len(combined.split()) <= max_words:
            buffer = combined
        else:
            if buffer:
                chunks.append(buffer)
            if len(section.split()) > max_words:
                # Fixed-size fallback for oversized sections
                words = section.split()
                for i in range(0, len(words), max_words - overlap):
                    chunks.append(" ".join(words[i:i + max_words]))
                buffer = ""
            else:
                buffer = section.strip()

    if buffer:
        chunks.append(buffer)

    return [c for c in chunks if len(c.split()) >= 20]
```

## Chunking Parameters

| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| `max_words` | 500 | ~650 tokens; fits embedding model limits |
| `overlap` | 75 | Prevents context loss at chunk boundaries |
| Min chunk size | 20 words | Discard trivial header-only fragments |
| Expected chunks/doc | ~26 | 11,071 ÷ (500 − 75) ≈ 26 |

## Metadata per Chunk

Every chunk carries full provenance metadata for cited answers:

```{python}
chunk_record = {
    "chunk_id":    42,
    "text":        "...",             # chunk content
    "doc_id":      "Italy_2013_CRPD_C_ITA_1",
    "country":     "Italy",
    "year":        2013,
    "doc_type":    "state report",
    "region":      "Europe",
    "symbol":      "CRPD/C/ITA/1",
    "source_file": "Italy_2013_CRPD_C_ITA_1.pdf",
    "chunk_index": 3,                 # position within document
}
```

---

# Step 4 — Vector Embeddings

## What Embeddings Do

An embedding model converts a chunk of text into a dense numerical vector (~768 numbers)
that encodes its semantic meaning. Chunks with similar meaning have vectors that are
geometrically close — enabling **semantic search**: a user's question is embedded into
the same space, and the nearest chunks are retrieved regardless of exact word overlap.

**Example of what this unlocks:**

> **Query:** *"barriers to accessing courts for people with disabilities"*
>
> **Retrieved:** chunks containing *"procedural accommodations"*, *"legal aid for disabled
> persons"*, *"access to justice mechanisms"* — even if none of those phrases appear in
> the query.

This is fundamentally superior to the current regex keyword matching.

## Embedding Model Options

All models below run **locally and for free** via `sentence-transformers`. No API cost.

### `all-mpnet-base-v2` — Recommended {.badge-green}

```{python}
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")
embeddings = model.encode(texts, batch_size=64, show_progress_bar=True)
# Shape: (N_chunks, 768)
```

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Dimensions | 768 |
| Quality | Highest for English text |
| Speed | ~2,000 chunks/minute on CPU |
| Max input | 384 tokens (~290 words) |
| Cost | Free (local) |

---

### `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` — Strong Alternative {.badge-blue}

Fine-tuned specifically for **question-answering retrieval** on 215M Q&A pairs.
Outperforms `all-mpnet-base-v2` on asymmetric search (short question → long passage).
Worth testing side-by-side on 20–30 sample queries.

```{python}
model = SentenceTransformer("multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1")
```

---

### `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` — Fastest Option {.badge-yellow}

6× faster, half the storage. Use for rapid prototyping or if Posit Cloud CPU is
a constraint. Switch to mpnet for production.

```{python}
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
# 384 dims, ~15,000 chunks/minute on CPU
```

---

### Model Comparison

| Model | Dims | Quality | Speed (CPU) | Cost | Use |
|-------|:----:|:-------:|:-----------:|:----:|-----|
| **all-mpnet-base-v2** | 768 | ★★★★★ | ★★★☆☆ | Free | **Default** |
| **multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1** | 768 | ★★★★★ | ★★★☆☆ | Free | Q&A focus |
| all-MiniLM-L6-v2 | 384 | ★★★★☆ | ★★★★★ | Free | Prototyping |
| OpenAI text-embedding-3-small | 1,536 | ★★★★★ | N/A | ~$0.15/corpus | Optional |

---

# Step 5 — Vector Store (FAISS)

FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is an open-source in-process library. The index is a
single binary file on disk, loaded once at app startup. No server, no Docker, no network
latency.

```{python}
import faiss
import numpy as np

# Build (offline, once)
dim = embeddings.shape[1]               # 768
faiss.normalize_L2(embeddings)
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)          # cosine similarity via inner product
index.add(embeddings)
faiss.write_index(index, "data/faiss_index.bin")

# Search (online, per query — < 5ms for 15,000 chunks)
query_vec = model.encode(["your question"])
faiss.normalize_L2(query_vec)
scores, indices = index.search(query_vec, k=8)   # top 8 chunks
```

## Vector Store Comparison

| Store | Type | Metadata Filter | Overhead | Recommended |
|-------|------|:--------------:|:--------:|:-----------:|
| **FAISS** | In-process file | Post-filter | None | **Yes — default** |
| ChromaDB | In-process DB | Built-in | Low | If filtering is critical |
| LanceDB | In-process DB | Built-in | Low | At larger scale |
| Pinecone / Qdrant | Cloud API | Built-in | High | Not needed |

At 15,000 chunks, FAISS exact search completes in under 5 milliseconds. Approximate
search methods (IVF, HNSW) add complexity with no meaningful speed benefit at this scale.

---

# Step 6 — LLM Selection

## Role in the RAG Pipeline

The LLM receives:

1. A **system prompt** defining its role, constraints, and tone
2. A **data context block** summarizing the current dashboard filter state
3. **Retrieved document chunks** from FAISS search
4. The **user's question**

It does not memorize the corpus — it reasons over the chunks provided per query.

## Recommended: Ollama llama3 (Local) + Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile (Groq API)

Ollama llama3 runs entirely locally with no API cost, making it ideal for high-volume
summarization tasks. Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile (Groq API) provides strong performance
on formal legal and policy text with reliable instruction-following at zero cost on the
free tier (30 req/min, 14,400 req/day), suitable for chat and report generation.
The entire stack runs at **$0/month** — Ollama local + Groq free tier.

### Model Routing Strategy

| Task | Model | Reason |
|------|-------|--------|
| Auto-summary panels | Ollama `llama3` (local) | Free; fast; simple summarization; no data leaves machine |
| Chat Q&A | Groq `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` | Best quality; free tier (30 req/min) |
| RAG deep Q&A | Groq `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` | High quality; free tier |
| Policy brief generation | Groq `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` | Strong quality for reports; free tier |

### System Prompt

```{python}
CRPD_SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
You are an expert research analyst for the Institute on Disability and Public
Policy (IDPP) at American University. You assist researchers, policymakers,
and advocates analyzing CRPD reporting data.

Rules:
- Answer ONLY using the data context and document excerpts provided.
- Never invent statistics, country names, or document references.
- Cite specific countries, years, and document types when referencing findings.
- If context is insufficient to answer, say so explicitly.
- Use formal, accessible language appropriate for policy audiences.
- Frame answers through a disability rights lens — the shift from medical
  to rights-based models is a central theme of this research.
"""
```

---

# Step 7 — New Dependencies

Add to `requirements.txt`:

```
# LLM & RAG
groq>=0.13.0
ollama>=0.4.0
sentence-transformers>=3.0.0
faiss-cpu>=1.8.0
pymupdf4llm>=0.0.17
```

Add to `.streamlit/secrets.toml` (git-ignored):

```toml
GROQ_API_KEY = "gsk_..."
```

---

# Step 8 — Phased Development Plan

## Phase 1 — AI Insights Panel

**Timeline:** Week 1 &nbsp;|&nbsp; **Prerequisite:** Ollama installed locally

Add an "AI Insights" button to the Overview tab. When clicked, Ollama llama3 receives
a statistical summary of the current filtered data and returns a plain-English narrative
— 3–4 bullet points describing what the current view shows.

- [ ] Add `groq` and `ollama` to `requirements.txt`
- [ ] Create `.streamlit/secrets.toml` with `GROQ_API_KEY`
- [ ] Create `src/llm.py` with `get_client`, `build_data_context`, `ask_llm`
- [ ] Add AI Insights expander to `tab_overview.py`

**User experience:** *One button click → instant AI narrative of any filter state.*

---

## Phase 2 — Conversational Chat Interface

**Timeline:** Week 2 &nbsp;|&nbsp; **Prerequisite:** Phase 1

**Status: ✅ Complete (2026-03-18)**

::: {.callout-note}
## Architectural Decision — Standalone Page (not embedded in Analyze tab)

**Original design:** The chat interface was planned as a `"💬 AI Research Assistant"` radio
option inside `tab_analyze.py`, alongside Article Coverage, Model Shift, and other analysis
views.

**As built:** The AI Research Assistant is a **top-level standalone page** at `?page=chat`,
routed from `app.py` via `elif page == "chat": tab_chat.render(df_all)`. It has its own
navbar entry (`smart_toy` icon) and is not embedded in the Analysis tab.

**Reason for change:** Better UX — the chat interface benefits from the full screen width
and an uncluttered layout. Embedding it as a radio option inside tab_analyze would have
constrained the layout and buried it under filter sidebar state. The standalone page uses
`df_all` (the complete, unfiltered dataset) — consistent with the intent that the chat
assistant works across the full corpus regardless of active filter state.

**Affected files:**
- `src/tab_chat.py` — new standalone module (not a modification of `tab_analyze.py`)
- `app.py` — routes `page == "chat"` to `tab_chat.render(df_all)`
- `src/nav.py` — top-level navbar entry with `smart_toy` icon
:::

Users ask free-form questions; Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile answers using live aggregated
statistics from the full (unfiltered) DataFrame. No RAG yet — answers are based on data
summaries only.

- [x] Implement `st.chat_input` + `st.session_state.chat_history`
- [x] Add multi-turn support via `ask_llm_multiturn`
- [x] Add example starter questions to guide users
- [x] Built as standalone page `src/tab_chat.py` (intentional departure from embedded radio design)

**Example questions this enables:**

> *"Which region shows the highest growth in rights-based language?"*
>
> *"Are there countries that submitted State Reports but never received Concluding Observations?"*

**Evaluation results (2026-03-19):** 13 questions (incl. 4 multi-turn turns), 0 errors.
Overall avg 4.92/5.0 (PASS). Rate limit guard: PASS. Out-of-scope refusal: PASS.
Multi-turn context retention: PASS.

---

## Phase 3 — Semantic Search & RAG

**Timeline:** Weeks 3–4 &nbsp;|&nbsp; **Prerequisite:** PDF files + Phase 2

This is the core upgrade. Chat answers become grounded in actual document text.

- [ ] Locate and place 585 source PDFs in `data/pdfs/`
- [ ] Run `LLM_Development/download_pdfs.py` if PDFs are not available
- [ ] Install `pymupdf4llm`, `sentence-transformers`, `faiss-cpu`
- [ ] Run `LLM_Development/build_knowledge_base.py` (one-time, ~20 min)
- [ ] Add `semantic_search` and `rag_answer` to `src/llm.py`
- [ ] Upgrade Phase 2 chat to use RAG: question → FAISS → Groq + evidence
- [ ] Display retrieved source citations below each answer
- [ ] Add semantic search to the Document Explorer sub-tab

**User experience:** *Answers now cite real document excerpts with country, year, and type.*

---

## Phase 4 — Policy Brief Generation

**Timeline:** Week 5 &nbsp;|&nbsp; **Prerequisite:** Phase 3

One-click generation of structured, export-ready research briefs from any filter state.

- [x] Add `generate_policy_brief` to `src/llm.py` (supersedes `generate_report`)
- [x] ~~Add "Generate Research Brief" button to the Document Explorer~~ → **Approved change (2026-03-19):** Implemented as standalone `src/tab_brief.py` page (`?page=brief`) with dedicated left/right panel layout and region-aware country scoping. Approved at design stage.
- [x] ~~Stream the output for responsive UX~~ → **Approved change (2026-03-19):** Spinner used instead of streaming — acceptable for brief-length output; streaming adds complexity without meaningful UX gain at this output size.
- [x] ~~Add download buttons for Markdown and plain text~~ → **Approved change (2026-03-19):** Export removed — reserved for paid subscribers (subscription feature).

**User experience:** *Select a region or country + articles + date range → click ✦ Generate Brief → view structured 4-section brief (Context, Key Findings, Recommendations, Sources) with generation stats.*

---

## Phase 5 — Evaluation & Quality Assurance

**Timeline:** Ongoing from Phase 3 &nbsp;|&nbsp; **Prerequisite:** Phase 3

Systematic measurement of retrieval quality and answer accuracy to guide iteration.

::: {.callout-important}
## Required Deliverables — Evaluation Scripts

Every evaluation in Phase 5 must produce **two Python scripts**:

1. **`LLM_Development/evaluate_phase4.py`** — runs all evaluation metrics end-to-end and saves results to `LLM_Development/eval_results_phase4.json`
2. **`LLM_Development/visualize_phase4.py`** — reads the JSON results and produces interactive **Plotly charts** saved to `LLM_Development/charts/`

These scripts must be committed and reproducible. Running `python evaluate_phase4.py` followed by `python visualize_phase4.py` must work from scratch with no manual steps.
:::

### Required Evaluation Scripts

#### `evaluate_phase4.py`

Must implement all of the following and write results to `eval_results_phase4.json`:

- [ ] Run all test questions from `LLM_Development/test_questions_phase3.jsonl` through the full RAG pipeline
- [ ] Compute **MRR**, **nDCG@10**, **Recall@5**, **Recall@10**, **Precision@5** across all retrieval queries
- [ ] Score each answer 1–5 on **Accuracy**, **Completeness**, **Relevance**, **Groundedness** using LLM-as-Judge
- [ ] Record **latency (ms)** per query
- [ ] Run evaluation across **all configured models** (see Cross-Model Comparison below)
- [ ] Emit a `"winner"` key and `"recommendation"` string in the output JSON

#### `visualize_phase4.py`

Must produce and save the following **6 Plotly charts** to `LLM_Development/charts/`.

Charts are grouped into two evaluation lenses that must be visually distinct:

- **IR / RAG lens** — measures retrieval pipeline quality (FAISS + embeddings): did we surface the right chunks?
- **LLM-as-Judge lens** — measures answer quality using Groq llama-3.3-70b as the judge: was the final generated answer accurate, grounded, and complete?

These two lenses can diverge. A config may retrieve excellent chunks (high IR scores) but still produce poor answers (low LLM-as-Judge scores), or vice versa. The charts must make this comparison visible.

##### IR / RAG Charts (Retrieval Pipeline)

| Chart | File | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bar: IR metrics per config | `phase4_ir_metrics.html` | `px.bar` (grouped) | MRR, nDCG@10, Recall@5, Recall@10, Precision@5 per model config; reference lines at targets (MRR 0.70, nDCG 0.80) |
| Scatter: IR score vs latency | `phase4_ir_latency.html` | `px.scatter` | x = avg retrieval latency (ms), y = MRR; color = config; size = nDCG — shows whether faster configs sacrifice retrieval quality |

##### LLM-as-Judge Charts (Answer Quality — Groq llama-3.3-70b evaluator)

| Chart | File | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bar: answer quality per config | `phase4_judge_quality.html` | `px.bar` (grouped) | Accuracy, Completeness, Relevance, Groundedness (1–5) per model config; reference line at 3.5 (pass threshold) |
| Heatmap: per-question scores | `phase4_judge_heatmap.html` | `px.imshow` | Rows = test questions, cols = dimensions; reveals which questions each config struggles with |

##### Cross-Lens Comparison Charts

| Chart | File | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radar: IR vs Judge per config | `phase4_radar_ir_vs_judge.html` | `go.Scatterpolar` | Two overlapping polygons per config — IR metrics (normalized 0–1) vs LLM-as-Judge scores (normalized 0–1) — shows alignment or divergence between retrieval and answer quality |
| Scatter: IR score vs Judge score | `phase4_scatter_ir_vs_judge.html` | `px.scatter` | x = avg IR score (MRR), y = avg LLM-as-Judge score; one point per config; color = config; annotations flag configs where retrieval and answer quality diverge |

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why the IR vs Judge comparison matters

If config_B (MiniLM embeddings) shows high LLM-as-Judge scores but low MRR, it means the LLM recovered well despite poor retrieval — suggesting the chunks it did find were sufficient. Conversely, if config_A retrieves perfectly (high MRR) but judge scores are low, the problem lies in the generation step (prompt, model, or context window), not retrieval. The cross-lens scatter is the most actionable chart for diagnosing where to improve.
:::

All charts must use the project color theme (UN Blue `#005bbb`) and be WCAG 2.2 compliant.

### Cross-Model Comparison Framework

`evaluate_phase4.py` must test **at least two model configurations** and compare them:

| Config ID | Embedding Model | LLM | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `config_A` | `all-mpnet-base-v2` (768-dim) | `groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile` | **Current production — baseline** |
| `config_B` | `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (384-dim) | `groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile` | Smaller/faster embeddings |
| `config_C` | `all-mpnet-base-v2` (768-dim) | `ollama/llama3` (local) | Local LLM fallback |
| `config_D` | `all-mpnet-base-v2` (768-dim) | `groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile` + query rewriting | Advanced RAG technique |

Output format in `eval_results_phase4.json`:

```python
{
  "run_date": "YYYY-MM-DD",
  "configs": {
    "config_A": {
      "embedding_model": "all-mpnet-base-v2",
      "llm": "groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
      "scores": {
        "accuracy": 4.75, "relevance": 4.625, "groundedness": 4.875,
        "completeness": 4.5, "mrr": 0.82, "ndcg": 0.87,
        "avg_latency_ms": 1240
      },
      "per_question": [...]
    }
  },
  "winner": "config_A",
  "recommendation": "..."
}
```

### Acceptance Criteria (PM sign-off required before Phase 5 is marked Complete)

- [ ] `evaluate_phase4.py` runs end-to-end with zero errors
- [ ] `eval_results_phase4.json` produced and committed
- [ ] All 6 Plotly charts generated in `LLM_Development/charts/` (2 IR, 2 LLM-as-Judge, 2 cross-lens)
- [ ] At least one config reaches **MRR > 0.70** and **nDCG > 0.80**
- [ ] At least one config reaches **overall answer quality ≥ 3.5/5.0** across all dimensions
- [ ] `ruff check . && ruff format --check .` passes with zero errors

---

### Build CRPD Test Set

- [ ] Create a JSONL file of 50+ human-written question–answer pairs across categories:
  - **Single-article lookup** — e.g., "What does Article 24 say about inclusive education?"
  - **Cross-country comparison** — e.g., "How do Rwanda and Kenya differ on Article 9 accessibility?"
  - **Temporal trends** — e.g., "How has reporting on Article 19 changed between 2010 and 2025?"
  - **Rights vs medical framing** — e.g., "Which countries still use predominantly medical-model language?"
  - **Multi-document synthesis** — e.g., "What are common gaps across all State Party initial reports?"
  - **Numerical/statistical** — e.g., "How many countries have submitted more than one report?"
- [ ] Include expected source documents (country, year, type) for each question
- [ ] Tag each question by difficulty (easy, medium, hard) and category

### Retrieval Evaluation

- [ ] Measure **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)** — how quickly the correct chunk appears in results (target: > 0.7)
- [ ] Measure **nDCG** (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) — overall ranking quality (target: > 0.8)
- [ ] Measure **Keyword Coverage** — do retrieved chunks contain expected key terms? (target: > 0.6)
- [ ] Log retrieval results per test question for before/after comparison when tuning

### Answer Quality Evaluation (LLM-as-Judge)

- [ ] Use a separate Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile call to score each answer 1–5 on:
  - **Accuracy** — Is the answer factually correct given the source documents?
  - **Completeness** — Does it cover all aspects of the question?
  - **Relevance** — Does it directly address what was asked?
  - **Groundedness** — Is every claim supported by a retrieved chunk? (hallucination detection)
- [ ] Flag any answer where a cited article, country, or year does not appear in the retrieved sources

### Human Evaluation Baseline

- [ ] Have 2–3 team members independently answer 20 test questions using only the source documents
- [ ] Use human answers as ground truth to calibrate the LLM-as-judge scores
- [ ] Identify question categories where human and LLM-judge scores diverge

### Advanced Retrieval Techniques (conditional on evaluation results)

- [ ] **Query rewriting** — Use Ollama llama3 to reformulate vague user questions before retrieval
- [ ] **Reranking** — Use Ollama llama3 to reorder top-k retrieved chunks by true relevance before generation
- [ ] **Multi-pass retrieval** — Run query variants and merge/deduplicate results for higher recall
- [ ] Only implement each technique if evaluation metrics show room for improvement

### Ongoing Monitoring

- [ ] Log all production queries, retrieved chunks, and answers for periodic review
- [ ] Track answer quality metrics weekly during initial rollout
- [ ] Set up alerts for retrieval failures (zero results or very low similarity scores)

**User experience:** *The system improves over time; quality is measurable, not anecdotal.*

---

## Phase Timeline Overview

| Phase | Feature | Week | LLM Model | Cost/Query |
|-------|---------|:----:|-----------|:----------:|
| 1 | AI Insights panel | 1 | Ollama llama3 (local) | Free |
| 2 | Chat Q&A (stats context) | 2 | Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile | Free |
| 3 | RAG chat + semantic search | 3–4 | Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile | Free |
| 4 | Policy brief generation | 5 | Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile | Free |
| 5 | Evaluation & quality assurance | Ongoing | Ollama llama3 (judge) | Free |

---

## Development Workflow Per Phase

Each phase follows a structured pipeline rather than jumping straight to code:

| Step | Activity | Output |
|:----:|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Requirements** | Problem definition and acceptance criteria (plan documents) |
| 2 | **Design** | UI mockups in Pencil for all user-facing components |
| 3 | **Infrastructure** | Backend logic — embeddings, FAISS, API calls, data pipelines |
| 4 | **Integration** | Wire backend into Streamlit using the design as spec |
| 5 | **Evaluate** | Run Phase 5 metrics against test set; iterate if needed |

- [ ] Design Phase 1 UI (AI Insights panel) in Pencil before coding
- [ ] Design Phase 2 UI (Chat Q&A interface) in Pencil before coding
- [ ] Design Phase 3 UI (semantic search bar, source citations) in Pencil before coding
- [ ] Design Phase 4 UI (report generation button, download panel) in Pencil before coding

Designing the UI before generating code ensures consistency with the existing dashboard (UN Blue theme, WCAG 2.2, Plotly charts) and reduces iteration cycles.

---

# Cost Management

## Strategies

| Strategy | Implementation |
|----------|---------------|
| Model routing | Ollama llama3 for summaries (free, local); Groq for chat and reports (free tier) |
| Response caching | `@st.cache_data` on deterministic calls |
| Chunk truncation | Cap each retrieved chunk at 600 words before inclusion |
| Retrieval limit | Default top-6 chunks; never more than 10 |
| Local embeddings | `sentence-transformers` runs locally — zero API cost |
| Session soft-cap | Track calls per session in `st.session_state`; warn at 20 |

## Estimated Monthly Cost

Assuming ~50 active users/month, 10 queries/user:

| Operation | Volume | Estimated Cost |
|-----------|-------:|:-------------:|
| AI Insights (Ollama llama3) | 500 calls | Free (local) |
| Chat Q&A (Groq) | 300 calls | Free (free tier: 30 req/min, 14,400 req/day) |
| RAG Q&A (Groq) | 200 calls | Free |
| Report Generation (Groq) | 50 calls | Free |
| **Total** | | **$0/month** |

---

# Security Considerations

| Concern | Mitigation |
|---------|-----------|
| API key exposure | `secrets.toml` stores `GROQ_API_KEY`; never committed; git-ignored |
| Prompt injection | User input never interpolated into system prompt |
| Cost runaway | Session-level call counter + `st.cache_data`; Groq free tier has built-in rate limits |
| Data in transit — summaries | Ollama runs locally; no data leaves the machine for summary generation |
| Data in transit — chat/reports | All Groq API calls are HTTPS |
| Document privacy | CRPD documents are public UN records — no PII concern |

---

# Scripts Reference

| Script | Location | Purpose | Run |
|--------|----------|---------|-----|
| `download_pdfs.py` | `LLM_Development/` | Initial download of 585 PDFs | Once |
| `sync_new_documents.py` | `LLM_Development/` | Monthly detection + download of new documents | Monthly (auto via GitHub Actions) |
| `build_knowledge_base.py` | `LLM_Development/` | PDF → Markdown → chunks → FAISS index | Once, then after each sync |
| `sync_crpd.yml` | `.github/workflows/` | GitHub Actions schedule for auto-sync | Automated |

---

# Implementation Status (as of March 2026)

This section tracks the current state of each component against the plan.

## Infrastructure Status

| Component | Status | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| `crpd_reports.csv` | ✅ Complete | 585 documents, 155 countries, 2010–2026 |
| `data/pdfs/` | 🟡 Partial | 27 of 585 PDFs downloaded |
| `data/markdown/` | ❌ Not started | Requires PDFs first |
| `data/embeddings.npy` | ❌ Not started | Requires markdown conversion first |
| `data/faiss_index.bin` | ❌ Not started | Requires embeddings first |
| `data/chunks_metadata.json` | ❌ Not started | Requires chunking first |
| `.streamlit/secrets.toml` | ✅ Complete | GROQ_API_KEY configured |
| `src/llm.py` | ✅ Complete | `get_client`, `build_data_context`, `ask_llm`, `ask_llm_multiturn`, rate limit guard |

## Script Status

| Script | Status | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| `LLM_Development/download_pdfs.py` | ❌ Not created | Planned; symbol-to-URL logic defined |
| `LLM_Development/sync_new_documents.py` | ❌ Not created | Planned; UN Digital Library API endpoint identified |
| `LLM_Development/build_knowledge_base.py` | ❌ Not created | Full code outlined in LLM_Integration_Plan.qmd |
| `.github/workflows/sync_crpd.yml` | ✅ Exists | Monthly sync workflow defined |

## Phase Status

| Phase | Feature | Status | Notes |
|-------|---------|--------|-------|
| 1 | AI Insights panel | ✅ Complete (2026-03-18) | `src/llm.py` + `tab_overview.py`; eval avg 3.58/5.0 |
| 2 | Chat Q&A interface | ✅ Complete (2026-03-18) | Standalone page `tab_chat.py` at `?page=chat`; eval avg 4.92/5.0 |
| 3 | RAG + semantic search | ❌ Not started | Depends on PDF collection + Phase 2 |
| 4 | Policy brief generation | ❌ Not started | Depends on Phase 3 |
| 5 | Evaluation & quality assurance | ❌ Not started | Depends on Phase 3 |

## Next Steps

Phases 1 and 2 are complete. The next milestone is Phase 3 (RAG + Semantic Search).

1. **Design Phase 3 UI** — Create Pencil mockup for semantic search bar and source citations panel in `LLM_Development/designs/`
2. **Complete PDF collection** — Run `LLM_Development/download_pdfs.py` to download remaining PDFs (27 of 585 downloaded)
3. **Build knowledge base** — Run `LLM_Development/build_knowledge_base.py` once PDFs are ready (PDF → Markdown → chunks → FAISS)
4. **Infrastructure** — Add `semantic_search` and `rag_answer` to `src/llm.py`
5. **Integration** — Upgrade `tab_chat.py` chat to use RAG; add semantic search to Document Explorer

---

# Decision Summary

| Decision | Choice | Reason |
|----------|--------|--------|
| Document source | UN Treaty Body Database + UN Digital Library API | Only available sources |
| PDF converter | `pymupdf4llm` | Best speed/quality for digital PDFs |
| Fallback converter | `docling` | Best quality if pymupdf4llm insufficient |
| Chunking | Semantic (by Markdown section) | Coherent, self-contained chunks |
| Chunk size | 500 words / 75 overlap | Fits embedding limits; sufficient context |
| Embedding model | `all-mpnet-base-v2` | Best English quality; free; local |
| Vector store | FAISS `IndexFlatIP` | Zero infrastructure; fast at 15K chunks |
| LLM | Ollama llama3 (local) + Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile (Groq API) | Free local summaries; free Groq tier for chat/reports |
| Model routing | Ollama for summaries; Groq for chat and reports | $0/month — both tiers free |
| Scheduling | GitHub Actions (monthly) | No infrastructure; commits automatically |
| Deployment | Posit Cloud (existing) | No infrastructure changes required |

---

# Appendix — Glossary

**RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)**
: A pattern where relevant documents are retrieved from a knowledge base and included in the
LLM's prompt, grounding its answers in real source material rather than training knowledge alone.

**Embedding**
: A dense numerical vector representing the semantic meaning of a text segment. Semantically
similar texts have embeddings that are geometrically close.

**Chunk**
: A fixed-size or semantically-bounded segment of a document. The unit of indexing and
retrieval in the RAG pipeline.

**FAISS**
: Facebook AI Similarity Search. An open-source library for efficient nearest-neighbor
search over dense vectors. Used as the vector store.

**Cosine Similarity**
: A measure of similarity between two vectors based on the angle between them. Used to
rank retrieved chunks by relevance to a query.

**Sentence Transformers**
: A Python library providing pre-trained models for generating text embeddings. All models
used in this plan are free and run locally with no API calls.

**UN Digital Library API**
: A documented JSON search API at `digitallibrary.un.org` that indexes all UN documents
and supports programmatic search by symbol prefix, sorted by date.
